result.markdown:
---
title: "A First Look at Hartford"
---



The scenario question from chapter 0: **Is it getting worse in Hartford?**

The data is loaded. Now let's answer it.

## Setup


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
library(dplyr)
library(readr)
library(here)

covid_data <- read_csv(here("data", "raw", "ct_covid_by_town_2026-06-30.csv"))
```
:::




## The pipe

Before diving in, one piece of syntax you'll see throughout this book:


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
# The pipe |> passes the result of one step into the next.
# Read it as "and then."
covid_data |>
  filter(Town == "Hartford")

# is the same as:
filter(covid_data, Town == "Hartford")
```
:::


Coming from SAS, think of it like chaining `DATA` steps and `PROC` steps
without writing an intermediate dataset between each one.

::: {.callout-tip}
## Keyboard shortcuts

| Shortcut | What it does |
|---|---|
| `Ctrl+Shift+M` | Insert the pipe `|>` |
| `Ctrl+Enter` | Run the current line or selection |
| `Ctrl+Shift+Enter` | Run the entire current chunk |

Learn all three early â€” you will use them on every line you write.
:::

## Step 1 â€” Filter to Hartford

`filter()` keeps only the rows that match a condition.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
hartford <-
  covid_data |>
  filter(Town == "Hartford")

nrow(hartford)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] 605
```


:::
:::


## Step 2 â€” Select and rename columns

`glimpse()` shows that some column names have spaces â€” a common headache
with real-world data. `select()` lets us pick the columns we want and
rename them at the same time.

Column names with spaces need backticks when you reference them:


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
hartford <-
  hartford |>
  select(
    date = `Last update date`,
    town = Town,
    new_positives = `Number of positives`,
    total_cases = `Total cases`,
    total_deaths = `Total deaths`
  )

glimpse(hartford)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
Rows: 605
Columns: 5
$ date          <chr> "06/24/2022", "06/23/2022", "06/22/2022", "06/21/2022", â€¦
$ town          <chr> "Hartford", "Hartford", "Hartford", "Hartford", "Hartforâ€¦
$ new_positives <dbl> 42545, 42519, 42499, 42467, 42451, 42383, 42345, 42327, â€¦
$ total_cases   <dbl> 33844, 33822, 33814, 33798, 33789, 33742, 33714, 33698, â€¦
$ total_deaths  <dbl> 417, 417, 417, 417, 417, 417, 417, 417, 417, 417, 417, 4â€¦
```


:::
:::


## Step 3 â€” Parse the date

The date column came in as a character string. We need it as an actual
date so we can sort and eventually plot it correctly.

`mutate()` adds or modifies columns. `parse_date()` from `readr` converts
a character string to a date given a format string.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
hartford <-
  hartford |>
  mutate(
    date = parse_date(
      date,
      format = "%m/%d/%Y"
    )
  )

glimpse(hartford)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
Rows: 605
Columns: 5
$ date          <date> 2022-06-24, 2022-06-23, 2022-06-22, 2022-06-21, 2022-06â€¦
$ town          <chr> "Hartford", "Hartford", "Hartford", "Hartford", "Hartforâ€¦
$ new_positives <dbl> 42545, 42519, 42499, 42467, 42451, 42383, 42345, 42327, â€¦
$ total_cases   <dbl> 33844, 33822, 33814, 33798, 33789, 33742, 33714, 33698, â€¦
$ total_deaths  <dbl> 417, 417, 417, 417, 417, 417, 417, 417, 417, 417, 417, 4â€¦
```


:::
:::


## Step 4 â€” Sort by date

`arrange()` sorts rows. The data comes in reverse-chronological order;
let's flip it so the earliest dates come first.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
hartford <-
  hartford |>
  arrange(date)

head(hartford)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
# A tibble: 6 Ă— 5
  date       town     new_positives total_cases total_deaths
  <date>     <chr>            <dbl>       <dbl>        <dbl>
1 2020-03-24 Hartford            NA          14            0
2 2020-03-25 Hartford            NA          20            0
3 2020-03-26 Hartford            NA          21            0
4 2020-03-27 Hartford            NA          21            0
5 2020-03-28 Hartford            NA          28            0
6 2020-03-29 Hartford            NA          39            0
```


:::
:::


## Putting it together

Each step above modified `hartford` and saved it back. We can write that
entire chain as one pipeline:


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
hartford <-
  covid_data |>
  filter(Town == "Hartford") |>
  select(
    date = `Last update date`,
    town = Town,
    new_positives = `Number of positives`,
    total_cases = `Total cases`,
    total_deaths = `Total deaths`
  ) |>
  mutate(date = parse_date(date, format = "%m/%d/%Y")) |>
  arrange(date)
```
:::


## What does the data say?


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
tail(hartford, 14)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
# A tibble: 14 Ă— 5
   date       town     new_positives total_cases total_deaths
   <date>     <chr>            <dbl>       <dbl>        <dbl>
 1 2022-06-07 Hartford         42099       33542          417
 2 2022-06-08 Hartford         42126       33560          417
 3 2022-06-09 Hartford         42154       33574          417
 4 2022-06-10 Hartford         42185       33601          417
 5 2022-06-13 Hartford         42255       33645          417
 6 2022-06-14 Hartford         42286       33673          417
 7 2022-06-15 Hartford         42327       33698          417
 8 2022-06-16 Hartford         42345       33714          417
 9 2022-06-17 Hartford         42383       33742          417
10 2022-06-20 Hartford         42451       33789          417
11 2022-06-21 Hartford         42467       33798          417
12 2022-06-22 Hartford         42499       33814          417
13 2022-06-23 Hartford         42519       33822          417
14 2022-06-24 Hartford         42545       33844          417
```


:::
:::


Two weeks of Hartford data, with new daily positives and running totals.
This is the foundation the briefing will be built on â€” clean, sorted,
and scoped to the question.

Note that `total_cases` is cumulative: it only goes up. `new_positives`
is the count of new positive tests reported for that date â€” that is the
number to watch when asking whether things are getting worse.
